schools.summary: The church at Antioch had five named prophets and teachers — Barnabas, Simeon Niger, Lucius of Cyrene, Manaen (Herod's foster-brother), and Saul — a varied company of different nations; the first Christian church (11:26) becoming the cradle of the Gentile mission.
Henry: the diversity of the gifted company, united in the ministry of the word; Manaen's connection with Herod showing the gospel's reach into high circles.

themes: Barnabas, Simeon, Lucius, Manaen, and Saul
the cradle of the Gentile mission

schools.summary: As they ministered (priestly worship, Robertson) and fasted, the Spirit said 'Separate me Barnabas and Saul' — designating, not ordaining, for the specific Gentile mission to which he had already called them (Barnes).

Calvin: the testimony to the Spirit's deity — he claims the right to choose ministers by his own authority, proving he is God, and (against Sabellius) a true person, for a bare epithet could not 'say' this (so Cyril).

themes: the Holy Ghost said, Separate me Barnabas and Saul
ministered to the Lord and fasted
the Spirit's call to the mission

schools.summary: The church, with fasting and prayer, laid hands on Barnabas and Saul and sent them — a solemn sending; the laying on of hands not ordination to the ministry (both had long preached) but a consecration to the specific missionary task (Barnes, Robertson, Wesley).
Calvin: the laying on of hands a consecration — the church offering them to God and declaring the work enjoined by him; the calling God's alone, the external setting-apart the church's.

debates.question: Was the laying on of hands an ordination to the ministry or a missionary consecration?

sides.position: A consecration/designation to the specific missionary task, not ordination (both already preached)

themes: fasted and prayed, and laid their hands on them

schools.summary: Barnabas and Saul, 'sent forth by the Holy Ghost,' departed to Seleucia (Antioch's port) and sailed to Cyprus (Barnabas's native island, 4:36) — the church's sending being the Spirit's sending (Chrysostom); the journey beginning naturally with Cyprus.

themes: being sent forth by the Holy Ghost

schools.summary: At Salamis they preached in the synagogues — Paul's rule of 'to the Jew first' (Robertson, Romans 1:16), the synagogues their base; John Mark as their attendant (hyperetes), helping in the work, perhaps baptizing converts (Ellicott).
Calvin: though sent to the Gentiles, it was meet to begin with the Jews; John Mark's help making his later desertion the less excusable.

themes: preached in the synagogues of the Jews

schools.summary: They crossed the whole island to Paphos (the western seat of the proconsul, famed for the temple of Venus, Barnes) and found Bar-jesus, a Jewish sorcerer and false prophet — one of the numerous Jewish magicians exploiting Gentile credulity, trading on their race's prestige (Robertson, Ellicott).
Calvin: the goodness of God in bringing the gospel even to Paphos, the filthy seat of idolatry.

themes: gone through the isle unto Paphos
a sorcerer, a false prophet, a Jew

schools.summary: Luke's term 'deputy' (proconsul) historically precise — Cyprus transferred to senatorial control under Augustus, with inscriptions/coins confirming a proconsul Paulus (Robertson, Ellicott), vindicating Luke against the charge of error; Sergius Paulus a prudent, open man desiring the word (Barnes, Clarke).
Calvin: the proconsul's seeking a providential preparation — weary of superstition, he desired a purer knowledge of God though he knew him not yet.

themes: the deputy Sergius Paulus, a prudent man
desired to hear the word of God

schools.summary: 'Elymas' the title interpreting his claim to wisdom (Arabic Ulema, sage, Barnes, Ellicott); he withstood the apostles, seeking to turn the proconsul from the faith, moved by self-interest, fearing the loss of his influence (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: Satan's opposition to the light — the seducer seeks to put away the gospel that exposes his darkness, as false teachers shut the eyes of the simple.

themes: seeking to turn away the deputy from the faith

schools.summary: First use of 'Paul'; 'filled with the Holy Ghost' a sudden access of power — insight, indignation, prevision of judgment (Ellicott, Robertson); the name change marking the transition to the Gentile mission with Paul as leader.

debates.question: Why is Saul now called Paul?

sides.position: He always had both names (Jew and Roman citizen); Luke now uses the Gentile name as Paul turns to the Gentiles
He assumed/adopted the name in connection with the conversion of Sergius Paulus

themes: Saul, who also is called Paul

schools.summary: Paul's Spirit-inspired indictment of devastating accuracy — 'full of all subtilty and mischief, child of the devil, enemy of all righteousness'; the threefold 'all' summing up total depravity (Robertson); 'child of the devil' a pointed contrast to his name Bar-jesus ('son of Jesus,' Ellicott).
Calvin defends the vehemence as Spirit-led — the proper way to deal with desperate enemies who stop the way before others; yet warns teachers against fleshly anger under colour of zeal.

themes: O full of all subtilty and mischief
perverting the right ways of the Lord

schools.summary: The judicial blindness a miraculous divine judgment (Robertson), evidence of Paul's inspiration, morally fitting — blindness for the man who fought against light and darkened others (Furneaux, Ellicott); 'for a season' marking the mercy, designed to lead to repentance (Barnes), with a tradition of his conversion.

Calvin: the hand of God inflicting the blow through Paul; a fit punishment, the sorcerer who darkened the sun cast into darkness — a perpetual example of God's wrath against those who resist the gospel.

themes: the hand of the Lord is upon thee
thou shalt be blind for a season

schools.summary: The proconsul believed, astonished at the doctrine — convinced both by the miracle (proving Paul sent of God) and the teaching (Robertson); the witnesses defend the genuineness of his saving faith against skepticism (Barnes, Robertson vs Renan), evidence the gospel reached the great and intelligent.
Calvin: the miracle the door to faith — the reverence of doctrine its beginning; the evident token of God's power broke the sorcerer's snare and opened his heart.

themes: astonished at the doctrine of the Lord

schools.summary: 'Paul and his company' marks Paul's emerging leadership (Luke now names him first, Barnabas accepting the secondary place, Robertson, Ellicott); John Mark's departure, for reasons unknown but blameworthy (Barnes, Acts 15:38) — fear, homesickness, or unwillingness to accept Paul's leadership — the seed of the later breach (15:38).

themes: the seed of the later breach

schools.summary: The missionaries journeyed inland to Antioch of Pisidia (a highland city, distinct from Syrian Antioch) and entered the synagogue on the sabbath, taking their seats — following their regular method of using the synagogue as the base for preaching Christ (Henry); the setting for Paul's first recorded full sermon.

themes: went into the synagogue on the sabbath

schools.summary: After the reading of law and prophets, the rulers invited any 'word of exhortation' — the custom of inviting a distinguished visitor (Robertson); Ellicott notes the coincidence fixing the sabbath (Paul echoing Deuteronomy 1 and Isaiah 1, the lessons just read).

Calvin: the teacher's office is to apply the Scripture to the people's present use, not to utter new things of his own; Clarke notes 'exhortation' may mean consolation, the Messiah being the Consolation of Israel.

themes: after the reading of the law and the prophets
if ye have any word of exhortation, say on

schools.summary: Paul stood, beckoned, and addressed two classes — 'Men of Israel' (Jews) and 'ye that fear God' (God-fearing Gentiles); the graphic touches suggesting an eyewitness report (Ellicott); the first full report of a Pauline sermon, his theology's keynotes appearing (Robertson).
Chrysostom: Paul's tactful address, first showing regard ('ye that fear God') before beginning; Calvin outlines the sermon's structure — Israel's history, Jesus the Christ, his office, and a warning.

themes: Men of Israel, and ye that fear God

schools.summary: Paul follows Stephen's method (ch. 7) of rehearsing Israel's history, the theme Israelites loved (Ellicott); 'chose our fathers' and 'with an high arm' (the exodus) figuring God's electing choice and mighty power (Barnes); these verses the sum of the Old Testament (Wesley).
Calvin: the free electing love — Israel chosen not for worthiness but by God's free love of Abraham, the fountain of all the benefits that followed.

themes: the God of this people chose our fathers
brought them out with an high arm

schools.summary: A textual variant turns on one letter: 'suffered their manners' (bore with their stubbornness) or 'nourished them' (as a nurse, Deuteronomy 1:31); Barnes, Clarke, Ellicott, Robertson prefer 'nourished,' fitting the conciliatory tone (God's mercies, not Israel's faults).
Calvin (reading 'suffered'): God's patience with a stubborn people, his electing love striving with their wickedness.

debates.question: Does the text read 'suffered their manners' or 'nourished them'?

sides.position: 'Nourished them' (as a nurse, Deuteronomy 1:31) — better MSS, fits the conciliatory tone
'Suffered their manners' — God's patience with the stubborn people

schools.summary: God destroyed (subdued, not all slain, Barnes) the seven nations of Canaan (Deuteronomy 7:1, the commonly named seven, Clarke) and divided the land by lot — the distribution in Joshua (the better reading 'gave for an inheritance,' Robertson); the fulfillment of the promise to the fathers.

The 450 years crux (apparent conflict with 1 Kings 6:1's 480) resolved: the true text places the 450 before 'he gave judges,' reckoning from the fathers/Isaac to the conquest (Robertson, Ellicott, Clarke); or Paul used the common reckoning (Josephus, Barnes), or round numbers — not impugning the history.
Calvin: God's patience through the judges' period, pardoning Israel's many backslidings, saving the nation alive amid many deaths.

debates.question: How is the '450 years' of the judges reconciled with 1 Kings 6:1?

sides.position: The true text reckons the 450 from the fathers/Isaac to the conquest, not over the judges
Paul used the common reckoning of his day (agreeing with Josephus) / round numbers

schools.summary: Israel desired a king (a rejection of the theocracy, 1 Samuel 8, Robertson); God gave Saul of Benjamin for forty years (confirmed by Josephus, perhaps including Samuel's judgeship, Barnes, Wesley); Paul, himself of Benjamin and once named Saul, dwells on the first king (Ellicott, Philippians 3:5).
Calvin: God's overruling — the people's wicked desire for a king became the occasion for establishing the kingdom from which Christ would come.

themes: God gave them Saul of Benjamin

schools.summary: God removed Saul (for disobedience) and raised David, 'a man after mine own heart' (a composite quotation, Psalm 89:20; 1 Samuel 13:14, Ellicott); explained of David's public, royal character — ruling by God's will unlike Saul — not his private sinlessness (Barnes, Clarke); Wesley warns against applying it to all his behaviour (Uriah).
Calvin: God respected the continual course of David's life and set him forth for Christ's sake — the title pointing beyond David to the true King after God's heart.

debates.question: In what sense was David 'a man after God's own heart'?

sides.position: His public, royal character — ruling by God's will, not his private sinlessness
The continual course of his life, and for Christ's sake (pointing beyond David)

themes: he raised up David to be their king
a man after mine own heart

schools.summary: The climax — 'of this man's seed [David's] God raised a Saviour, Jesus,' according to the long-standing promise (2 Samuel 7; Psalm 132; Isaiah 11; Jeremiah 23, Robertson); Paul goes no further than David because David suggests Jesus, his descendant in the flesh.
Calvin: in sending Christ God had respect to his own faithfulness — he sent him because he had promised; the name 'Jesus' (Savior) declaring what he performs.

themes: of this man's seed God raised a Saviour
Jesus, the Savior of David's line

schools.summary: John the Baptist, the forerunner, preached the baptism of repentance before Christ's coming — heralding the king (Robertson, Malachi 3:1), his baptism characterized by repentance (Mark 1:4; Luke 3:3).

Calvin: Paul cites John to prove he preached the true Christ whom the famous forerunner commended — John, esteemed a prophet by all, a powerful witness; his baptism a token of the great alteration at hand.

John, fulfilling his course (his ministry a race, Robertson), bore self-effacing witness — 'I am not he... whose shoes I am not worthy to loose'; the proverbial figure of utmost lowliness; Robertson marvels at Paul's accurate knowledge of John's words (Mark 1:7; Luke 3:16).
Calvin: John's testimony all the more credible for refusing the honor offered him, abasing himself lest his greatness darken Christ's glory — the model witness pointing wholly to Christ.

themes: whose shoes I am not worthy to loose

schools.summary: Paul's direct application — addressing again the two classes, Jews ('children of the stock of Abraham') and God-fearing Gentiles ('whosoever feareth God'); 'to you is the word of this salvation sent' — the gospel of the Savior Jesus (v.23), offered first to the Jews (Barnes), resting on the work of Jesus (Ellicott).

themes: children of the stock of Abraham, and whosoever feareth God
to you is the word of this salvation sent
the personal offer of the gospel

schools.summary: Gill: Paul speaks of those really ignorant of Christ (not all, for some knew yet rejected him), pleading their ignorance, choosing not to provoke; the dreadful irony — the Jews, not knowing Christ nor understanding the prophets read every sabbath, fulfilled those prophecies by condemning him; the providence overruling their ignorance and malice to accomplish the foretold death (Acts 2:23); the warning that men may handle Scripture yet be ignorant of its meaning, fulfilling it against themselves.

themes: they knew him not, nor the prophets

schools.summary: Barnes: they found no crime deserving death — conclusively shown by the trial (after Judas's treason, false witnesses, all their efforts, no crime laid to his charge); the Sanhedrim, unable to substantiate blasphemy before Pilate, sought his death on the ground of sedition (the charges shifting, none proven); yet they demanded his death — the guiltless condemned by malice, not justice; Christ's established innocence (1 Peter 2:22), the injustice the measure of his enemies' malice and proof of his sinlessness.

themes: they found no cause of death in him
desired Pilate that he should be slain

schools.summary: Gill details the fulfilled prophecies — the reproaches, buffeting, scourging, piercing of hands and feet (Psalm 22:16), crucifixion between thieves (Isaiah 53:12), parting of the raiment (Psalm 22:18), piercing of the side (Zechariah 12:10); Barnes: the taking down and burial done by Jews (Joseph, Nicodemus), the burial fulfilling Isaiah 53:9; all that was written fulfilled to the letter, the certainty of his death established (the ground for the resurrection), the providence overruling the enemies' deeds to accomplish the prophecies.

themes: fulfilled all that was written of him
took him down from the tree

schools.summary: Gill: though killed by man and certainly dead and buried, Christ could not be held by death's cords (Acts 2:24) but God raised him by his power; Clarke: the resurrection the fullest proof of his innocence (God alone raises the dead, not but on an extraordinary occasion) — vindicating Christ, declaring him the Son of God (Romans 1:4); the great 'but' contrasting man's killing with God's raising, the divine reversal of the unjust condemnation, the resurrection the foundation of the gospel.

themes: but God raised him from the dead

schools.summary: Barnes: Christ seen 'many days' (forty, Acts 1:3) by the apostles, chosen to bear witness; Clarke: the resurrection recent, many witnesses still alive and ready to attest it; Ellicott: Paul's language that of one who had conversed with the witnesses and convinced himself of their testimony; the resurrection attested by chosen, qualified, living eyewitnesses (seen over many days, not a single sighting), the foundation of the gospel in their testimony — attested fact, not invention.

themes: seen many days of them from Galilee

schools.summary: Gill: the glad tidings = the whole gospel (incarnation, obedience, sufferings, death, resurrection of Christ, and the benefits — peace, pardon, righteousness, life, salvation); Barnes: 'the promise made unto the fathers' = all spoken in the OT of the Messiah (to Abraham, David, the prophets), nothing more grateful to a Jew than that the Messiah had come; the gospel the fulfilment of the ancient promise (no new thing, the continuity of the gospel with the OT hope), good news, the joy of the promise fulfilled.

themes: we declare unto you glad tidings
the promise made unto the fathers

schools.summary: Lightfoot discusses the second psalm (Psalm 2:7); 'raised up Jesus again' variously read — God raising him up into the world (sending/manifesting the Messiah, as Acts 3:22; 13:23), or raising him from the dead (the resurrection, the surrounding theme); 'this day have I begotten thee' applied to his eternal generation, his incarnation, or (with Paul's own use, Romans 1:4) the resurrection as the declaration and 'begetting' of the Son (firstborn from the dead) — the resurrection-reading fitting the context (Paul proving the resurrection).

debates.question: What does 'raised up Jesus... this day have I begotten thee' mean?

sides.position: The resurrection — the day Christ was declared the Son of God with power (Romans 1:4), fitting Paul's context
God's raising him up into the world (sending/incarnating the Messiah), the begetting his eternal generation or incarnation

themes: God hath fulfilled the promise in Jesus
Thou art my Son, this day have I begotten thee

schools.summary: Lightfoot: 'the sure mercies of David' (Isaiah 55:3), by 'David' the Messiah understood (as the Rabbins Kimchi and Ibn Ezra acknowledged); Gill: a distinct article — the resurrection to die no more; Christ raised to incorruption, no more to return to corruption (Romans 6:9, death no more dominion); the sure mercies of David fulfilled in Christ — the surety of the covenant mercies requiring the ever-living Christ (they could not be sure if he died and remained dead), the believer's salvation resting on his abiding life.

themes: raised him up, no more to return to corruption

schools.summary: Barnes: the citation Psalm 16:10, to the same intent — to prove the Messiah must rise and live for ever (God declaring by David that his Holy One would not see corruption); Ellicott refers to Peter's fuller treatment (Acts 2:25-31, showing it could not refer to David but the Messiah); 'thine Holy One... shall not see corruption' — the Messiah's body raised before it decayed (the third day), the resurrection without corruption foretold and fulfilled; the consistency of the apostolic testimony (Peter and Paul both citing Psalm 16).

themes: thou shalt not suffer thine Holy One to see corruption
the Holy One not seeing corruption

schools.summary: Barnes: the verse shows Psalm 16 could not refer to David (who served his generation, died, was buried, and saw corruption) but must relate to the Messiah; David, the psalm's writer, did see corruption, so the prophecy of one not seeing corruption must refer to Christ, raised before corruption (v.37) — the argument (as Peter's, Acts 2:29-31) establishing the psalm speaks of Christ; 'served his own generation by the will of God' a noble epitaph, the believer's calling to serve God's purpose in his day.

themes: David served his generation, fell on sleep
serving one's generation by God's will

schools.summary: Barnes: the Lord Jesus raised without undergoing the change that succeeds death — since David returned to corruption and Christ did not, the passage in Psalm 16 referred to the Messiah; Gill: the subject the Lord Jesus, raised by God the Father; the decisive conclusion (Christ saw no corruption, his body not decaying, raised the third day), the prophecy fulfilled in him, his Messiahship proved from Scripture, the foundation laid for the application — forgiveness and justification (v.38).

themes: he whom God raised saw no corruption

schools.summary: Gill: Paul, having discoursed of Christ's incarnation, death, and resurrection, proceeds to the benefits — forgiveness of sins (v.38) and justification (v.39) — published in the gospel; Barnes: the benefits of his death now stated; forgiveness 'through this man' (through Christ, his death the ground, his name the channel, Ephesians 1:7), the great benefit of the gospel (the pardon of the guilty), preached and offered to all (Luke 24:47); the 'therefore' grounding the offer in the proven resurrection and Messiahship.

themes: through this man is preached forgiveness of sins
the great benefit of the gospel

schools.summary: Gill: Christ's righteousness imputed is the matter of justification (not the works of the law, nor obedience, nor faith as a work, but Christ the object of faith); justification by Christ complete and perfect — 'from all things' (all sin of every kind, all the law can charge, all God's justice can demand); the law of Moses could not justify (it condemns but cannot justify, Romans 3:20; Galatians 2:16); the great Pauline doctrine of free justification by faith in Christ, the gospel's answer to the works of the law.

sides.position: By faith in Christ, through his imputed righteousness, complete from all things — not by the works of the law (which cannot justify)

themes: all that believe are justified from all things
which ye could not be justified by the law

schools.summary: Gill: the warning against rejecting the gospel and its truths (forgiveness and justifying righteousness to be had no other way than through Christ); Barnes: the more needful because the Jews held themselves safe (secure in their descent and the law) — the false security making the caution necessary; to reject the gospel (the only way of salvation) is to incur the judgment foretold by the prophets (Habakkuk 1:5, v.41); the balance of the gracious offer (vv.38-39) and the solemn warning, the gospel both gracious and solemn.

themes: beware lest that come upon you
the warning against rejecting the gospel

schools.summary: Gill discusses the rendering of 'despisers' (Habakkuk 1:5; the Septuagint 'despisers,' which Paul follows; the Hebrew variously, the Syriac 'ye impudent,' the Arabic 'ye negligent'); in Habakkuk the warning of the Chaldean invasion (a work the people would not believe), applied by Paul to the work of God in Christ and the gospel; the despisers to 'wonder and perish' (astonished yet ruined in their unbelief), refusing to believe the wonderful work though plainly declared — the peril of rejecting the gospel.

themes: behold, ye despisers, and wonder, and perish
a work ye shall in no wise believe

schools.summary: Chrysostom marks Paul's wisdom — he gained admiration and put into the hearers a longing for a second hearing, dropping seeds and forbearing to solve all the questions, his plan to interest and engage their good-will, not make them listless by casting all at once; the Gentiles especially besought that the words be preached again the next sabbath (the eager desire, the hunger for the word stirred); good preaching stirring a longing for more, the Gentiles' readiness foreshadowing the turning to them (v.46).

themes: besought that these words be preached again
the eager desire for a second hearing

schools.summary: Clarke: 'many of the Jews' (descendants of the tribes) and 'religious proselytes' (heathen converted to Judaism) followed the apostles; 'persuaded them to continue in the grace of God' = to continue to credit the gospel and receive its influence; Chrysostom marks the eagerness; the first converts at Antioch exhorted to perseverance (not merely to begin but to continue), to hold fast the grace of the gospel (forgiveness, justification) — the danger of falling away, the pastoral care establishing new converts in steadfastness.

themes: many of the Jews and proselytes followed
continue in the grace of God

schools.summary: Barnes: the news of the apostles' presence and doctrine circulated by the Gentiles who heard them, curiosity attracting the multitude; Clarke: the good news spread far and wide; almost the whole city (Jews and Gentiles) gathered to hear the word of God — the remarkable interest the gospel stirred, the drawing power of the word; the great concourse (especially of Gentiles) about to provoke the Jews' envy (v.45), the gospel's success stirring opposition; the providence drawing the multitude that many might believe (v.48).

themes: almost the whole city came together
to hear the word of God

schools.summary: Barnes: 'envy' (Greek zeal) here denotes wrath and indignation that such multitudes should hear a message they rejected, threatening their religion; the unbelieving Jews, seeing the multitudes drawn to the gospel, filled with envy (their influence eclipsed, the gospel prevailing — as 'for envy they delivered Christ,' Matthew 27:18); they 'spake against' Paul's doctrine, contradicting and blaspheming (reviling Jesus of Nazareth); the success of the gospel provoking the envy and opposition of its enemies, issuing in blasphemy.

themes: filled with envy at the multitudes

schools.summary: Barnes: the apostles 'waxed bold' (not terrified, the blasphemies showing alarmed consciences, the time to declare the truth more fearlessly); 'necessary' that the gospel be offered first to the Jews (Luke 24:47; Romans 1:16, 'to the Jew first'); the Jews, rejecting it, 'judge yourselves unworthy of everlasting life' — the self-condemnation of unbelief (by their own act, not God's verdict); 'lo, we turn to the Gentiles' — the Jews' rejection the occasion of the Gentiles' calling (Romans 11:11), the providence overruling unbelief to advance the gospel.

themes: it was necessary the word be spoken first to you
ye judge yourselves unworthy of everlasting life
lo, we turn to the Gentiles

schools.summary: Barnes: Paul appeals to the Scriptures (Isaiah 49:6) rather than Christ's command, because the Jews recognized their own Scriptures while scorning Jesus of Nazareth's word; the passage refers to the Messiah's mission to the Gentiles; Gill connects it also to Christ's commission to all nations (Matthew 28:19); 'a light of the Gentiles... salvation unto the ends of the earth' — the Gentile mission foretold and commanded (no afterthought), the apostles fulfilling the prophecy, the gospel's universal scope to the farthest reaches.

themes: so hath the Lord commanded us
salvation unto the ends of the earth

schools.summary: Barnes: the Gentiles glad that salvation was no longer confined to the Jews, glorifying the word (receiving it as from God); 'as many as were ordained to eternal life believed' — the natural reading (the Greek, Syriac 'destined,' Vulgate) of divine ordination/appointment, the believing the fruit of God's electing ordination (Romans 8:30, election issuing in faith); some offer 'disposed/inclined,' but the word and natural sense point to divine ordination, not mere human disposition — salvation of God, faith his gift.

debates.question: What does 'ordained to eternal life' mean?

sides.position: Divine ordination/appointment to life — election issuing in faith (the natural reading)
'Disposed' or 'inclined' to eternal life — a human disposition (offered by some)

themes: the Gentiles were glad, glorified the word
as many as were ordained to eternal life believed

schools.summary: Gill: the word published throughout all the region of Pisidia, not by Paul and Barnabas (who went on to Iconium) but by the new converts gifted to preach; many churches planted, the church at Antioch in Pisidia continuing for centuries (a later patriarchate and metropolitan); the gospel expanding from the city to the surrounding region, carried on by the converts (becoming evangelists); the providence spreading the word despite the Jews' opposition, every believer a witness, the lasting fruit of the mission.

themes: the word of the Lord published throughout the region
the lasting fruit of the mission

schools.summary: Gill: the 'devout and honourable women' Gentile women proselyted to Judaism, religious and of distinction, whose husbands were chief men of the city, the Jews working through them upon their husbands; the unbelieving Jews, unable to refute the gospel, stirring up the influential (the prominent women and city leaders) to raise persecution and expel the apostles; the malice that, lacking truth, employs power and influence; the irony of 'devout' women made instruments of persecution (John 16:2), the world's enmity against the truth.

themes: stirred up the devout and honourable women
malice that cannot answer the gospel

schools.summary: Clarke: shaking off the dust a significant rite — in effect saying 'ye are worse than the heathen, your land accursed for opposing God, we dare not let its dust cleave to our feet'; done per Christ's command (Matthew 10:14; Mark 6:11; Luke 9:5) 'for a testimony against you, that we offered you salvation but ye rejected it'; the solemn renunciation marking the rejecters' guilt and the apostles' part done; obedience to Christ's instruction, the apostles undeterred, moving on to Iconium.

themes: shook off the dust of their feet against them

schools.summary: Gill: 'the disciples' the new converts at Antioch (or the apostles), filled with joy at the gospel and at being counted worthy to suffer, the joy and the Holy Ghost joined; Barnes: this joy in the midst of persecution, an evidence that the gospel can fill the soul with joy in the severest trials; the joy a fruit of the Spirit (Galatians 5:22), flowing from the Holy Ghost (not nature or circumstance), triumphing over trial (Romans 12:12); the chapter ending with joy and the Spirit despite the persecution, the gospel's triumph.

themes: the disciples were filled with joy
the gospel's joy a fruit of the Spirit
